summary: Graham explains that Y Combinator wasn’t started primarily for money or altruism, though both are present in small ways. The core motivation was a hacker’s impulse: a small, well-placed intervention could unlock many new startups. He frames YC as a clever hack to make the world’s economic engine run more efficiently.
key_points: YC was partly fun, like a Viaweb reunion, but not mainly about enjoyment.
Startups are usually a grind, so YC wasn’t launched for fun or lifestyle reasons.
The founders didn’t start YC mainly for money or charity; outcomes were uncertain.
YC is motivated by a hacker’s desire to apply a small force that triggers many new startups.
Startups are viewed as a net good, but the driving impulse is amoral tinkering.
money_quotes: We did it because it seems such a great hack.

There are thousands of smart people who could start companies and don't, and with a relatively small amount of force applied at just the right place, we can spring on the world a stream of new startups that might otherwise not have existed.

But really what motivates us is the completely amoral desire that would motivate any hacker who looked at some complex device and realized that with a tiny tweak he could make it run more efficiently.
In this case, the device is the world's economy, which fortunately happens to be open source.
key_insight: YC was created as a hacker-style lever—small, targeted effort to unlock a large wave of new startups—rather than for money or altruism.
